data.title: Medical Properties Trust: Why Basket Making Is Important
data.description:
Investors in Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW) were quick to point out the business concentration with Steward as an issue for the losses. But many of those same investors took a big position in Medical Properties Trust only to repeat the same result in their portfolio of investments. This article is about why I make baskets (because Medical Property Trust results happen). Even though things did not work out as planned, I want my portfolio to not reflect only the results here.

Medical Properties Trust appears to be through the worst. It would seem that uphill is what is left. However, management has quite a bit to prove to long-term shareholders that they have learned from the latest experience. For that reason, this is a recovery play. But it may not be a long term buy-and-hold type situation.

The last article noted that as a secured lender, the company had a high priority. Management made sure it had a claim to the high priority by providing part of the loan during the bankruptcy process. Despite all of management's assurances (including the debtor-in-possession financing or DIP loan) impairment charges occurred. Now these might come back to some extent (or possibly even completely) as management finishes the process of getting back to holding leases. But there is definitely no assurance that will happen.

Steward

Some have attributed much of what happened to the sizable business relationship with Steward. That is definitely possible.

But I would have to add that the leverage situation of Steward was not properly evaluated by management for the risks it entailed. In particular, the cash flow situation appears to have caught management by surprise on several occasions. At the very least, the covid challenges should have motivated management to start working with Steward on a solution back around 2020. This is something that should have been factored into the amount of business that was done with the company.

Combine that with the fact that when something did happen with Steward that eventually led to a liquidation situation, there was an evaluation failure as to the logistics that would be entailed with that much business to replace all at one time. Management was confident that a smooth transition would be assured. Obviously, that was not the case, and shareholders have seen the losses that have resulted so far.

Diversification Importance

This is one reason that I repeatedly advise investors to consider making baskets. For me, this investment was 2% of the portfolio. When I sold it for a loss, the dividends made up for that loss before looking at the rest of the portfolio.

But the rest of the basket is doing fine. There are some industry leaders in there like Simon (SPG) and Realty Income (O) that have done decently. But the big news is that another recovery idea, SL Green (SLG) has more than made up for the losses here and just those two together now show a small profit.

I control risk by putting "safe" or "safer" ideas in the basket as well as by keeping each idea to roughly 2% of the portfolio when I buy them. But I also control risk by deciding how much of the basket is in riskier and turnaround ideas.

I have always loved speculating and probably will continue to in the future. But as I have grown older, the portfolio has a lot more less risky ideas than it did when I was younger. For me, that 2% meant that if the idea worked out it would add a percentage or two to the portfolio return. But if, as in this case, the idea "turned left" and went "overboard" as some ideas will inevitably, then the basket overall should do what I have expected it to do. So far, that is exactly what is happening.

The other reason for diversification is that even though I am an oil and gas person, the best performing parts of my portfolio (this calendar year) are the banks and the REIT's. Except for a few issues, oil and gas is basically sitting this year out. But that is another thing that seems to happen, and it's another reason I am not all in one place at one time.

Upcoming Earnings Report

This third quarter report will likely have a lot of nonrecurring items. Hopefully management will give an update as to the remaining values of the remaining properties. I have a feeling that if management can spread out the remaining sales, then there will be better realized prices. Just gaining control of the hospitals so they were not subject to a court sales process alone was a major victory. It also should be expected for a secured lender.

The agreement that was recently announced probably has all the analysts out there reworking their models. However, I suspect some details affecting the remaining hospitals as far as the "gaining control" aspect will give everyone more information during the next earnings conference call. It seems like there are always some unanticipated costs in a situation like this (or maybe some legal twists and turns).

Management Goals

Management itself has got a lot of work to do to assure investors that something like this will not be happening again. For the business the company is in, financial issues with "the other side of the deal" is part of the business from time to time.

However, as some have noted, additional business needs to be accretive in nature. That was not always the case in the past. The market may also decide to make the company "live within its means" which means that the company cannot sell stock every time it wants to do a big deal. This may slow the restoration of business loss. But it may also result in a sounder model.

In general, management needs to demonstrate to the market it knows what to avoid in the future as well as to put the company back where it was but in a better financial position.

Business can be concentrated with a partner. But then, that partner had better be "bullet proof" because things happen, and the partner needs to survive those "things".

Summary

For me, this is a buy as a recovery play. It is very likely that the stock can recover a fair amount of lost ground now that the Steward issue is over with.

However, to make to the long-term buy-and-hold category, management has to prove that it learned was needed to not repeat what just happened. Until that is accomplished this will remain a higher risk play.

What does not concern me is how fast management went from being satisfied with Steward's progress to taking action to protect shareholders shortly afterwards. The reason is that leveraged partners have very little room for error. That accounts for the fast change in attitude by Medical Properties Trust management.

The problem instead was partnering with a highly leveraged partner who did not have the cash flow issues resolved in every possible situation. Combined that with the percentage of business done with this same partner. This is where management has work to do in reassuring shareholders.

Risks

Much of the uncertainty of the bankruptcy process is now over with. However, there is no assurance that the resolution of the hospitals (even those with sales anticipated) will be satisfactory to shareholders.

The Steward bankruptcy uncovered some "holes" in the company strategy that have led to some serious impairment charges so far. Management needs to demonstrate to shareholders that they can rebuild the company better so that these issues do not recur again. More importantly any rebuilding (and all rebuilding) needs to be accretive.

Rising interest rates could dampen the cost recovery hopes of the remaining properties if interest rates do rise. Similarly falling interest rates generally make anything that is real estate more valuable.

The loss of key personnel could really set back the company plans.

data.title: LVMH: Thesis Not Yet Broken, But Poised For Future Growth And Resilience
data.description:
I wrote about LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) (OTCPK:LVMUY) it in July of this year. Although not much time has passed, the stock has shown significant volatility. I believe that in the long term, the thesis remains unchanged, despite the company facing more challenges than I initially anticipated. This is just an example of how the short term constantly tests the patience of long-term investors, but if one knows how to interpret the news and events, it can definitely work in their favor.

This article will serve to review the quarterly results, the conference call and my thoughts on them.

A weak Q3 overall

Let’s be honest. The year 2024 hasn’t been particularly stellar for LVMH. The company is only experiencing positive growth in the perfume and selective retailing segments. We all know that the wines and spirits segment is the most cyclical in the group—something the management also acknowledges, and it’s a trend affecting the entire sector. However, we might start seeing light at the end of the tunnel in 2025, or at least that’s what Pernod Ricard's (OTCPK:PRNDY) (OTCPK:PDRDF) said in their latest earnings call.

What I find surprising, however, is the negative growth in the leather and goods segment. While there may be more aspirational brands within this segment, even Dior, their second most prominent brand, hasn’t escaped from negative growth this quarter. It’s also worth noting that currency exchange rates have had a negative impact across all segments, due to the strengthening of the euro against other currencies during the semester.

I don't believe the segment is facing a serious problem, beyond something temporary. Additionally, over these past months, we’ve seen how management is clearly handling their brand portfolio. An example of this is the sale of a brand that’s more in the premium segment rather than high-luxury, like Off White. The other big news is the small investment in one of the top companies in the sector, Moncler.

These moves undoubtedly reflect the continuous strategic thinking the leadership has regarding their brands. Anyone familiar with Bernard Arnault knows the CEO of LVMH is obsessed with brand preservation, scarcity, and status. In fact, I’ll delve deeper into this later, as it’s the key to this thesis.

That said, both the management team and I were surprised by the poor performance of the brand's most resilient segment, which, let’s not forget, boasts operating margins of 40%, nearly on par with Hermès.

One point that doesn’t concern me as much, but that should be mentioned, is the potential increase in taxes in France. The management addressed the concern with a stroke of the pen. However, there is increasing interventionism and socialism in Europe, and this issue will impact any company if we continue down this path.

"Finally, your question on taxes in France. First of all, roughly speaking, you should consider that France is about 40% of our pretax income, more or less. And -- no, sorry, 1/3 of our pretax income and 40% of our taxes. It's 7% of sales, 1/3 of pretax income and 40% of taxes. The impact of additional corporation taxes in France should range in between EUR 700 million and EUR 800 million." Jean-Jacques Guiony, CFO.

Another negative point, and the one that continues to worry investors the most, is the weakness in China (not to be confused with the Chinese consumer). The Asian region (excluding Japan) saw its decline accelerate to 16% QoQ, though we must remember that around this time last year, this region was growing in double digits, so the comparison isn’t straightforward. This continues to highlight the fragility of an economy that is being propped up by government stimulus. The Chinese economy is not in a strong position at the moment, but I’d argue there’s little doubt that this will change in the long term.

In fact, the good Jean-Jacques Guiony has a clear opinion on the matter:

"Yes. Things will never be the same again. I mean, it would be the first time that I don't hear that when things are going down. No, I don't believe that, I mean it. We are still very, very strong believers in the future of luxury, in the future of the emergence of upper middle class in China and elsewhere.

And we see absolutely no reason why after a cyclical downturn, as we are experiencing today, we shall not be in a position to recover. So it's normal that people are asking the question that this is totally structural and will never come back exactly the same way, maybe, but we are still very hopeful that the luxury industry will continue to develop and we'll continue to sort of swerve on the wave of the emergence of the upper middle class, as we've been doing over the last 55 years." Jean-Jacques Guiony, CFO.

The truth is that I haven’t been following the company for 55 years, but it’s definitely a situation that management has experienced several times before. Their response to analysts who only look at the next semester and forget the true thesis of LVMH strikes me as brilliant.

The rest of the geographies are showing some growth, and there are reasons to be optimistic, but clearly, China still plays a significant role in the narrative, even though it carries less weight in sales over time:

"Well, thank you, Chris. A little bit detailed, if I may, and we are not used to disclosing that level of details for obvious competitive reasons. What I would say about the Mainlanders is not that easy to measure because, as I said, I mean, about 45% of the business takes place outside China, and it's more difficult to measure outside China, whether we are talking about aspirational customers or other types, VIC or other types of customers. So we find it hard.

If you take the Japanese or the Americans, for instance, the bulk of their business, i.e., 95% or 90% takes place in their home country, which is not the case with Chinese. So analyzing the situation with -- I mean, where the drop comes from is a little bit tricky." Jean-Jacques Guiony, CFO.

From this, we can draw two conclusions. The first is that the Chinese consumer is much more important than the Chinese region itself, due to this curious dynamic in sales that the country follows. The second point I believe is that the wealthier Chinese consumers are the ones who travel and make purchases outside their country, leaving behind a more aspirational customer base in China. These customers are much more cyclical and less appealing. I appreciate that they are not succumbing to short-term pressures and have chosen to focus on what it is really important, the brand power.

The View Remains Focused On The Long Term

I connect the last sentence directly with this part.

For me, the management remains focused on preserving the brand's power and exclusivity in the long term, and that is what gives meaning to the thesis and value to the company. Furthermore, not everything is lost in China:

"However, the strength of Chinese demand in the first half of the year provides recent evidence of the enduring appetite of the Chinese customer for luxury, which makes us very confident that demand will recover when confidence troughs." Jean-Jacques Guiony, CFO.

"We've seen a lower level of business in China as Mainland China, as I explained. But we've seen also an enormous operating leverage over the past few years due to the fixed structure of the cost. And therefore, we can absorb this lower level of business without major difficulties. It will have a little bit of an impact on margins, but not in a tremendous way." Jean-Jacques Guiony, CFO.

In my opinion, all these risks regarding China are temporary issues. The desire for European luxury will continue to prevail in the region. The longing for status and exclusivity has been a part of human nature since the first men stored precious stones to collect and pass down as inheritance. This is something that will not change, and the Chinese want to be like Westerners as they continue to develop their economy.

Losing focus on brand image preservation by lowering prices, creating accessible collections, or buying more aspirational brands would pose a terminal risk to the thesis and, in my opinion, be a reason to sell. In this type of company, I am not looking to invest in a premium brand like Nike, but rather a luxury company like Hermès.

Fortunately, LVMH's management is one of the best at this. One only has to look at the great work they are doing with Tiffany & Co.

"We continue to progress on Tiffany. So now in terms of renovated stores with new concepts, we are above 25%. And on those stores, we are growing double digits. So there is a real uplift once we have renovated those stores on sales.

And the other pillar of the strategy, if you remember what Jean-Jacques shared with you, is around the icons and really pushing the icons. So as of now, the icons will represent 60% of the Jewelry. If we leave aside Bridal, Silver and High Jewelry, and they are growing as well. So overall, in Tiffany, also, and I will repeat what Jean-Jacques told you in H1. You shouldn't expect double-digit growth tomorrow.

But we are seeing sequential improvement, which is really the results of the execution of the strategy delivering. It takes time, it costs money and it takes investment, so you have to be patient. But overall, we are confident that, step-by-step, we are gradually improving on that." Cécile Cabanis, Deputy CFO.

Another point I appreciate is that, despite the difficult moment in the cycle, they are not cutting expenses in OPEX (R&D, marketing, or distribution). There are numerous examples of how these decisions end up being the right ones in the long term. Once again, it’s that business vision and desire to do things well, rather than succumbing to short-term pressures, that I like to see in the companies I invest in.

"First of all, emphasize product innovation. I know it's not the easiest growth driver for you to model, but it is one of the most impactful. And hopefully, we've demonstrated over the years that we have the skill set to harness the creativity of our talent pool and translate it into commercial success. It also happens to be one of the most cost-effective drivers, which I can only applaud as a CFO.

Secondly, we have consistently invested in our distribution network and in communication in the recent years. And although we need to continue to do so, we don't need to accelerate. So whilst we are reluctant to anticipate on cost control, if only to avoid self-fulfilling prophesies, we can and we will adjust our cost to what we see, as we have done many times in the past." Jean-Jacques Guiony, CFO.

Final Impressions

Overall, it hasn’t been a particularly desirable quarter, but as I’ve been able to demonstrate, the thesis is not broken, and it may emerge even stronger after all this, especially if smaller rivals with weaker brands, less scale, and less financial muscle are also facing similar problems.

I appreciate that they are not jeopardizing the brand's value, as this would pose a clear terminal value risk that could completely alter my investment thesis in the company. For now, I remain calm, focused on the long term, and pleased to see that I am in good hands.

Regarding the valuation, there is hardly any change compared to the previous article. Most of the value of this company lies beyond the first five years of cash flows.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Datadog: Waiting On 2 Catalysts - AI Is One Of Them
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Datadog (NASDAQ:DDOG), one of the fastest-growing cloud software companies, has been stubbornly stuck in a tight range so far in 2024.

The cloud software industry, of which Datadog is a part, continues to see tighter spending on cloud software products as most customers in this space are either allocating their IT budget on expanding capital investments in GenAI or on GenAI PoCs (proof-of-concepts).

Datadog has been no exception and reiterated some aspects of their customer environments in a host of conferences that its management participated in where they talked about how software companies are still not fully benefiting from expanding IT budgets just yet.

This has led to some stubborn range-bound action in Datadog’s stock, with the maker of data observability products up just 5.6% for the year.

There are early signs of IT budgets that are possibly opening up for Datadog, but for now, the lack of catalysts still makes Datadog a Hold. However, I have raised my price targets for Datadog, as shown in the valuation section below.

Discerning SMB customers, GenAI catalysts aren’t firing growth cylinders yet.

Despite having a fairly positive opinion on Datadog, its product suite, the company’s robust management team, and the ITOM/ITSM (Infrastructure Operations/Services Management) market it operates in, I have been neutral on Datadog through this year.

In my last coverage on Datadog, I had expressed moderate levels of optimism about the company’s GenAI-focused product portfolio and the initial traction the company had already started to see since the fourth quarter of last year.

What I deeply appreciate about Datadog’s management is their relentless focus on execution and the immense transparency where they willingly share updates on their targets and milestones with investors.

For example, they first started reporting on GenAI’s dollar contribution to the company’s top line in H2 of last year, where they said that the company’s “next-gen AI customers contributed about 2% of ARR.” In the most recent call, Datadog’s management said that, GenAI contribution accounted for 4% of the company’s total revenues. Those are extremely impressive growth rates, and when taken in context, implies GenAI revenue for Datadog grew 153% on a trailing twelve-month basis as compared to the company’s 27% revenue growth rates over the same time period. Investors should note that Datadog is one of the few software companies that breaks out its GenAI contribution to its top line in terms of dollar impacts.

But in the broader scheme of things, the low single-digit contribution that GenAI has had on the company so far is not moving the needle enough to push Datadog’s growth rates back in excess of 40% CAGR growth rates that it would usually post.

I agree with management views about GenAI being a catalyst, given the robust product roadmap the company already has in place to support customers as they scale their GenAI applications. But management has cautioned as recently as in the Communacopia conference where they think Datadog, like most other software companies, will be a late-stage beneficiary:

"We still are in a cost-conscious environment. I would say in comparing it to the hyperscalers, we tended to have higher growth rates than the hyperscalers. But the hyperscalers have timing differences and potentially business differences.

A good example is that, in order to deploy AI applications, you first have to invest in the infrastructure. And there have been significant beneficiaries, including the hyperscalers, Nvidia, et cetera. And we tend to benefit a little bit later in that cycle once those are put in applications. So you may have timing differences in what's accelerating the hyperscalers' growth and Datadog."

Outside of the company’s enterprise customers, Datadog’s mid-market and smaller customers are not exactly splashing budgets on Datadog’s product suite. Per Datadog’s 10-Q, the company has 3390 customers who contribute +$100k ARR, and this customer base has grown just 13% in the last twelve months. In another recent conference, management revealed that 2/3rd of the company’s customer base represents mid-market and SMB customers, and spending among these customers “hasn't accelerated to the degree that the enterprise [customer] has.”

Datadog’s dollar-based net retention rate, although robustly elevated, continues to marginally decelerate to the mid-110 percentage levels, indicating slowing spend among the company’s customers relative to last year. Until these levels pick up, it will be hard to be bullish on the company again in the near term.

Datadog's price target raised but upside remains muted.

With no major change seen yet in Datadog’s revenue prospects, I will keep my revenue outlook for Datadog relatively unchanged at 25% CAGR through FY26.

However, in terms of margins, the company has done an incredible job to expand margins so far. At a gross margin level, Datadog expanded margins rapidly from the 70s to the low 80s, as management alluded to at another recent conference and as can be seen in Exhibit B. At the same time, the company is also getting further efficient with its cost structure and has the potential to expand adj. operating margins to ~26% by FY26, which implies almost 30% of CAGR growth in earnings.

Assuming a discount rate of 8% and a share dilution rate of ~1.1%, the earnings growth implies an earnings multiple of ~60x, which points to 7% upside from current levels.

Risks & Other Factors To Look For

The most important factor for Datadog and most cloud software companies is the commentary about their views on their customer budgets and when they expect IT budget allocations to start to benefit cloud software companies such as Datadog. One of the first metrics one should observe closely is the dollar-based net retention metric, and any signs of expansion there would indicate spending environments are getting better for Datadog.

Takeaway

Without the strong presence of catalysts, Datadog will continue to be range-bound as cost-conscious mid-market and SMB customers show no signs of loosening their budgets for cloud software companies such as Datadog.

At the same time, GenAI contributions are growing rapidly for Datadog, but single-digit contributions are not going to make a big impact yet for Datadog, which makes the GenAI impact muted for Datadog.

For now, Datadog’s near-term prospects continue to be muted, leading me to reiterate my Hold rating for Datadog.

data.title: XLE: The Sector Has Not Performed Well In Recent Interest Rate Cut Cycles
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend selling the Energy Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLE). Fed rate-cutting cycles often precede recessions, and as we will see, cyclical sectors tend to outperform defensive sectors.

Additionally, the oil and gas sector in particular has averaged a -1.5% return in recent rate-cutting cycles in the US. Finally, XLE trades at a valuation above its 3-year historical average in both P/E and EV/EBITDA, which does not offer a good risk-reward ratio.

Energy Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF

Before I present my investment thesis, I want to introduce you to the Energy Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF. The objective of the ETF is to provide exposure to U.S. companies operating in the oil, gas, and renewable energy sectors. Below are the main holdings.

Created in 1998, the ETF currently invests in 22 companies and has a management fee of 0.09%, with over $32 billion in assets. Now, let's move on to the investment thesis. Enjoy your reading!

Fed Interest Rate Cuts Have Preceded Recessions

In my view, it is extremely important to analyze macroeconomic variables before making an investment. In this sense, we can see from the graph below that 4 of the last 5 interest rate cut cycles preceded a recession in America.

The XLE ETF gives us exposure to the oil, gas, and energy sectors, and we know that this sector is characterized by being cyclical. In this sense, how have cyclical sectors performed in a scenario of interest rate cuts in America?

As we can see, on average, companies in cyclical sectors have an average return of 1.8% 12 months after the first Fed interest rate cut, while companies in defensive sectors have a return of 4.6%, which corroborates my skeptical view.

But you could argue that cyclical sectors encompass more than just the oil and gas sector, so let's now look at the specific average return of the oil and gas sector over the past few Fed rates cut cycles.

When analyzing the average returns of each sector, we see that the returns of the energy sector (including oil) have an average return of -1.5%, being among the worst along with the financial segment, and media and entertainment, which corroborates my skeptical view.

Valuation Is Not Attractive

One of the best ways to value an ETF in my opinion is to use a comparative multiple and analyze its own history. In this sense, we will analyze the P/E and EV/EBITDA multiples, as we will see below.

When we look at EV/EBITDA, XLE traded at lows of 4.4x EBITDA and at highs of 7.2x EBITDA, for an average of 5.8x EBITDA. This implies a 17% downside to the current valuation of 7x EBITDA.

When we look at P/E, XLE traded at lows of 6.7x earnings and at highs of 15x earnings, for an average of 10.8x earnings. This implies a 25% downside to the current valuation of 14.5x earnings.

Being more conservative, I will use the EV/EBITDA multiple as the basis for my pricing, and based on a 17% downside, the fair value for XLE in my assessment is $75, which supports my recommendation to sell the ETF.

Furthermore, I feel quite confident about my recommendation when I analyze Seeking Alpha's quant tools. As we can see below, the tool's recommendation is also to sell the ETF, given the risks and momentum.

Potential Risks To The Bearish Thesis

As a risk to my bearish thesis, the American economy currently shows no clear signs of entering a recession; on the contrary, my assessment is that the American economy is experiencing a soft landing, showing considerable resilience.

Additionally, the data presented in this article are statistical averages from past scenarios similar to the current one. However, the current economy is completely different from the economy of the 1980s or 2000s. Therefore, there is no guarantee that the results will be repeated.

The thesis is complex, and investors should be very cautious in their analysis of the XLE ETF. In my view, ETFs deserve even more diligent analysis than stocks because they contain so many of them.

The Bottom Line

In this article, we have seen that interest rate cut cycles in America often precede recessions, and that cyclical sectors such as energy and oil have not performed well in these periods.

When we specifically analyze the XLE ETF, the valuation using the comparative multiple method does not provide a safety margin; on the contrary, there is an implicit downside of 17% for the ETF to return to its average over the last 3 years.

Based on this analysis, I recommend selling the Energy Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF. In my view, the historical data and the current valuation of the ETF only support the thesis of selling the asset.

data.title: Meta Platforms: It Is Not Time To Jump On To The AI Hype Train
data.description:
Introduction

Share price of Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) has surged by 67% on a year-to-date basis. This rally can be attributable to: (1) the company's consistently strong earnings in recent quarters and (2) the market exuberance driven by artificial intelligence. Although the company has proved its resilience and ability to capture growth, the ability to monetize artificial intelligence remains a huge uncertainty. In this report, I will demonstrate why the price of META is potentially undervalued. Investors should adopt a wait and see approach, gaining more color into META's artificial intelligence monetization strategy before making further decisions.

Latest Developments

In 2Q24, META posted yet another strong set of earnings. The social media giant generated $39.07 billion in total revenues, representing a 22.10% year-on-year growth. Advertising revenues increased by 21.68% to $38.72 billion. Reality labs revenue surged by 27.89% to $353 million. Overall gross margins remained relatively stable at 81.30%. During this period, net margin improved from 23.44% last year to 34.46% primarily due to reduced spend in R&D and SG&A. Capital expenditures for the firm remained elevated as the company continue to invest heavily into artificial intelligence; on a year-to-date basis, the company has spent $15.18 billion as compared to $13.44 last year.

Advertising Business To Maintain

Total advertising impressions have grown by 9% on average across all regions. Although growth is tapering, the advertising segment remains robust as supported by increasing growth in the firm's average price per advertisement, suggesting that the company not only has command of the market but also strong pricing power. Across all regions, growth of average price per ad surged to 10% year-on-year, as compared to -16% in 2Q23.

Outlook of the advertising industry remains strong despite a slowdown in marketing budgets. Based on a recent study conducted by Gartner, total marketing budgets fell by 15% as companies leverage on generative artificial intelligence. However, budget allocations to paid media (i.e., advertising) has increased from 25.60% to 27.90%. Allocations to other categories such as labor and marketing technology solutions are expected to deteriorate.

More importantly, META has a history of being agile and adaptable. The company has always taken risks to ensure that they stay on top of the market. Although some of these risks are questionable, most of the company's bets are paying off. For example, the company's more recent Threads and new features in Facebook (e.g. Reels) have shown success in maintaining the gen-z cohort.

In addition, we should expect to see META and GOOG to benefit from the exodus of advertisers from X (formerly twitter). According to a recent survey based on 18k consumers and 1k senior markets, Kantar found that 26% of marketers will be planning to reduce advertising on X. Moreover, X is tweaking its business model to focus on creators' engagements rather than advertising; it is likely that we will continue to see further reduction of ads in one of META's longtime competitor.

Artificial Intelligence & Reality Lab As Potential New Growth Engines

Currently, META has two major growth engines: (1) Artificial Intelligence and (2) AR/VR (i.e., Reality Labs). Although revenue from AR/VR segment has tapered, the industry is still in its nascent stages and META's is already an industry leader and monetizing it. As of 1Q24, the company holds at least 74% of the AR/VR headset market share.

According to Statista Market Insights, the AR/VR market size is likely to reach $51 billion by FY2026, with an average growth rate of 17% per annum. As the industry further matures in the consumer market, META is likely to be one of the major beneficiaries. In fact, as an industry leader, META has continued to make advances. On 25th September, the company introduced Orion, an AR glass that has the look and feel of a regular pair of glasses as compared to the bulkier Quest 3; Orion will be used as a prototype for the company to iterate and build more friendly consumer AR glasses. The industry just needs a "Chat-GPT" moment to introduce AR/VR to the mainstream.

On the artificial intelligence front, how will META monetize its capabilities remains a big question. Monetization will most likely come from either inference from API points or a consumer app similar to ChatGPT. However, at this stage, there is simply no color on how the company can monetize it. In fact, there are already arguments that the ROI from AI will not be able to cover the costs and investments made today.

Nevertheless, META remains one of the leading players in artificial intelligence. Although OpenAI's GPT-4 still outperforms META's Llama, the company's large language models is still one of the main models in the market as it is open-source for developers.

Current Share Price Implied Much Of META's Future Potential Has Been Priced In

On a year-to-date basis, the company has surged by 67%. Part of the reason is because the company has performed exceptionally well over the past four quarters. Revenues grew at an average rate of 24% year-on-year, while net income has surged at an average rate of 139% year-on-year. Net income growth was primarily due to a drastic reduction in SG&A; SG&A margin declined to an average 14.86% for the past four quarters from 21.42% in the preceding four quarters. However, on a forward-looking basis, how much growth is being priced in?

Based on my valuation model, if we assume: (1) margins stay constant as per the latest quarter, (2) current share price of $576.16, (3) terminal growth of 2%, and (4) WACC of 10.90%, markets are pricing in a 37.80% growth per annum for the next five years. For reference, META's revenue growth rates for the past ten quarters and four quarters are only 11% and 24% respectively; an annualized growth of close to 40% per year is extremely optimistic. One may argue that META's AI may potentially contribute to the company's revenue significantly; however, as noted above, it is better to wait until we have further color on how the company can monetize this segment.

This suggests that there is a low margin of safety for investors who are interested in taking new positions at the current price. Firstly, if META fails to meet expectations, it is likely we will see a huge correction. More importantly, META will have to exceed current expectations for new investors to benefit.

Key Elements To Watch In 3Q24

META is scheduled to report its 3Q24 earnings on 30th October 2024. We should closely monitor the company's capital expenditures. Previously, the company warned that costs are expected to rise further as the company invested heavily on servers and data centers. Although Amazon and Microsoft are also spending heavily on infrastructure, these companies have a dedicated business model around them (e.g. Microsoft Azure).

Additionally, we should continuously track the company's developments around artificial intelligence. Any new information on how the company is able to monetize its artificial intelligence capabilities will drastically change its growth outlook.

Finally, the company has beaten consensus EPS estimates consistently over the past four quarters. Currently, the consensus estimate for EPS is 5.28, representing a growth of 20.39% year-on-year. If META disappoints, it could signal that the company is facing operational challenges or headwinds which may be enduring in the short-run.

Closing Remarks

Overall, META continues to be a strong company that has an innovative spirit, strong fundamentals, a healthy balance sheet, and is able to generate sustainable cashflows. Apart from its strength in its advertising segment, the company has two potential growth engines that may be able to further expand its revenue significantly in the coming years.

Unfortunately, at the current share price, much has already been priced in by market participants; there is a low margin of safety for investors. While some may argue that the price is justifiable because of its potential in artificial intelligence, I believe it will be wise for investors to wait and see how META can potentially monetize its artificial intelligence capabilities instead of jumping onto the AI hype train.

data.title: Nvidia Stock: The Extraordinary Growth Should Slow Down Despite Blackwell
data.description:
A lot has happened since my last Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) stock coverage. The company has been praised many times here on Seeking Alpha for its Blackwell platform, which many analysts argue can further increase its already substantial growth rate. However, this excessive optimism seems to be exaggerated. Nvidia is already one of the most expensive companies in the world, and its stock is extremely overvalued. I will explain why in this article.

My previous analysis of Nvidia stock

In my previous article about Nvidia’s valuations, I wrote that NVDA stock was expensive compared to its peers according to valuation ratios. To the positive factors, I added that the company's profitability indicators were indeed high. In addition, Nvidia has recently had a great track record of exceeding analysts' earnings expectations. The problem is that these expectations have been rising for a while. Despite Nvidia being the market leader and enjoying its FAANG customers' loyalty, NVDA stock was not a great buy because it was expensive when I wrote the article.

My investment case for Nvidia remains broadly unchanged. However, I came to the idea to update my analysis on the corporation thanks to a lot of articles published here on Seeking Alpha describing the benefits of Blackwell for Nvidia’s success. Nothing has really changed, however. Blackwell chips were announced in March 2024, quite a while ago. But everyone predicts their imminent success and says Nvidia’s sales would be highly supported by them for a while. Let me analyze if that is true.

Nvidia’s Blackwell news

So, what exactly is Nvidia’s Blackwell, and why should it be so interesting to investors now in October 2024? Well, there was a press release published on 18 March 2024 saying that the NVIDIA Blackwell platform arrived. This platform enables organizations all over the world to build and run real-time generative AI on trillion-parameter large language models. Importantly, the platform can do so at up to 25x cheaper while consuming less energy compared to its predecessor. The Blackwell technologies are useful in areas such as data processing, engineering simulation, electronic design automation, computer-aided drug design, quantum computing, and generative AI.

According to Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang, “Blackwell is the engine to power this new industrial revolution. Working with the most dynamic companies in the world, we will realize the promise of AI for every industry.” Among the corporations expected to adopt Blackwell are Amazon Web Services (AMZN), Dell Technologies (DELL), Google (GOOG), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), OpenAI, Oracle (ORCL), Tesla (TSLA) and xAI. This indeed inspires hope of even greater sales and cash inflows for Nvidia.

As I have mentioned before, Mr. Huang is again calling the AI race the “industrial revolution”. The growth rate in this area is indeed very high. The diagram below, borrowed from my fellow analyst Oliver Rodzianko, shows how high the AI growth rate has been in the last two years.

But it feels like there are too much excitement and great expectations among investors. Although the Magnificent Seven is indeed buying Nvidia’s chips, the demand for these might eventually slow down. I am not sure how long this growth will last, but it would not be eternal and certainly, it will not always be as high as it is now.

Also, we should not forget that there is competition from other chipmakers, including Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). AMD has recently released its Instinct MI325X AI chip, which according to the management, outperforms Nvidia's H200 processor in specific AI tasks. AMD also plans to release the MI350 series, an alternative to Nvidia's Blackwell, in 2025. However, right now, Nvidia is still the market leader, but the question is how long this would last.

Nvidia’s fundamentals

Now, let us go to Nvidia’s financial fundamentals.

Nvidia is a highly profitable and cash-rich company with little debt. This has not changed compared to the time when my previous analysis was published. Its net debt is still negative, it can pay its interest on debt 237 times, while its current ratio suggests Nvidia has plenty of idle cash. From this perspective, Nvidia is highly unlikely to go bankrupt.

Nvidia’s quarterly earnings history

Now, let us have a look at Nvidia’s earnings history.

Nvidia’s sales and net income history (in $million)

Interestingly, since the April 2023 quarter, Nvidia’s sales and earnings have been rising at a fast pace. However, as can be seen from the net income graph above, the growth rate has recently slowed down. This is especially true of the April 2024 and July 2024 quarters. If the trend continues, it seems likely to me that the growth rate will slow down even further. Let us have a look at Nvidia’s outlook.

Nvidia’s earnings outlook

Nvidia is set to report its next quarterly earnings on 19 November 2024.

NVIDIA's earnings outlook for the third quarter of fiscal 2025 is the following: sales are expected to be $32.5 billion, plus or minus 2%. GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins are expected to be 74.4% and 75.0%, respectively, plus or minus 50 basis points. No EPS forecasts were given in the management’s outlook. However, it is likely Nvidia will beat its earnings estimates.

Only the expectations are growing substantially. Moreover, as can be seen from the diagram above, every single quarter the spread between the forecasted EPS and actual EPS is only getting lower. 4Q 2024 or the third quarter of fiscal 2025 will not be an exception, it seems.

Anyway, Nvidia is predicted to report $0.69 in EPS, somewhat more than the $0.65 reported on 28 August 2024. But the growth rate is slowing down.

It seems likely that if Nvidia’s growth rate slows down, investors’ enthusiasm will also decrease somewhat.

Apart from seeing the actual sales and EPS numbers, I would also like to listen to the management’s press conference, especially the company’s innovations, its position relative to competitors, the economic conditions, and the management’s outlook for the future quarters and years because it is unlikely the sales and earnings growth will last forever.

Nvidia’s valuations

As I have mentioned before, Nvidia is a highly overvalued company, not only relative to the S&P 500 companies but also compared to its close competitors.

NVDA stock is now trading at a P/E GAAP ratio (TTM) of 61.96, substantially higher than the industry’s average of 24.09. All the indicators, including the P/B, the P/CF, the EV/Sales, and the dividend yield suggest extreme overvaluation relative to the sector’s averages. Only the PEG indicator does not suggest overvaluation because it considers the earnings growth rate. As I have mentioned before, Nvidia’s growth rate is high but seems to be unsustainable.

Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), AMD (AMD), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Texas Instruments (TXN) all on average have lower valuation ratios than Nvidia. Only Nvidia’s P/E and PEG ratios are somewhat lower than AVGO’s and AMD’s.

In addition to the high actual valuation ratios, NVDA stock is also trading at its all-time highs.

So, I would say that NVDA stock is highly overvalued.

Upside factors

There are many risks to my rather cautious outlook on Nvidia. I am trying to be contrarian, while the company seems to be firing on all fronts. After all, its sales and its net profits are rising, while it regularly announces new products and technologies. Moreover, NVDA is a highly popular stock. Its loyal fans are willing to overpay for it to benefit from its exponential growth. Then, some stocks trade at a substantial premium to their peers for years, if not decades. A classical example of such a company is Amazon. Not only does the company still manage to boast good financial results, but it also has plenty of loyal investors who are willing to buy and hold its shares for a while. So, it is not worth saying that a stock like NVDA would collapse in value. That is why I would think twice before short-selling Nvidia’s shares. At the same time, it does not seem to be a good time for me to buy NVDA’s stock.

Downside risks

The downside risks are clear, in my view. To start with, NVDA shares are highly overvalued. These are not just trading at their all-time highs; these are much more expensive relative to peers. So, an investor buying a stake in Nvidia will not be enjoying a margin of safety. Moreover, Nvidia is currently enjoying very high sales growth, which only started in the April 2023 quarter. Normally, growth at a very high pace tends to be unsustainable. In addition, even though Nvidia is the market leader, it still has relatively strong competition from its rivals, including AMD and Intel (INTC). I wrote an article on the subject before. Finally, we should not forget that the leading stock indices, including the S&P 500, are trading near their all-time highs, suggesting that asset markets are somewhat overheated. Many economists are also saying a stock market correction is near. This means that most stocks, especially overvalued ones like Nvidia, are likely to plunge in value.

Conclusion

Overall, I consider Nvidia to be a highly overvalued company with brilliant, albeit slowing sales and earnings growth rates. The corporation is highly popular among investors and has leading market positions. But this does not mean it is a smart buy right now. The competition is getting tenser while the growth rate is slowing down. At the current price, you would not enjoy the necessary safety margin. So, my investment rating remains unchanged, namely "hold".

data.title: Centamin: Acquisition By AngloGold Ashanti Is Good News
data.description:
I last covered Centamin plc (OTCPK:CELTF) (TSX:CEE:CA) in August 2020 when it was trading at $2.68 and had a target price range of $3.20 to $3.50. As shown below, this has not been achieved as it is trading around $2.13.

This miner extracts gold in the Egyptian desert and was recently subject to a takeover by AngloGold Ashanti plc (NYSE:AU), valuing it at approximately $2.5 billion or 163 pence per share, which translates to around $2.13. I have a Hold position since the stock has already surged to this value, and, for shareholders, this thesis aims to show how Centamin's prospects have improved as it will now operate as part of the combined entity which puts it in a better position to face risks and expand production in countries where it has exploratory interests.

First, I provide some explanation as to why my buy position was wrong.

Churning Gold Out Of The Egyptian Desert

At that time, I was bullish on Centamin mainly because of its growth opportunities as the leading gold producer in Egypt since competitors like Canadian-based Aton Resources (OTCPK:ANLBF) were still at the exploration stage in a country with immense mineral resources, and where the mining sector was still at its infancy compared to others in Africa.

Additionally, it exhibited operational resilience as it had successfully maintained production levels during the initial phase of COVID-led disruptions in 2020 when authorities in Egypt were imposing drastic social distancing measures to contain the spread of the virus. By contrast, in other parts of the world, Yamana Gold (PAAS), a Canadian miner was finding it more difficult to sustain operations amid the pandemic, leading to service disruptions.

Centamin's resiliency resulted in higher production and with the Fed reducing interest rates in June 2020 to near zero (0.25%), precious metal prices got a boost, resulting in the revenue chart below trending upwards.

However, subsequently, asset prices retreated in 2021 explaining why revenue declined as per the above chart. Later, as of March 2022, the Fed embarked on an aggressive rate hike cycle favoring the greenback at the detriment of gold prices. Also, by that time other miners who had been impacted by the pandemic recovered, which explains why Centamin's outperformance of the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) was not sustained beyond 2020 as shown in the blue chart below, partly explaining why my bullish forecast did not materialize.

There is another reason for the stock not having appreciated as, despite the recent surge, it is still trading lower than in 2020, by around 30%. This has to do with the risks.

Valuation And Risks

Thus, when I covered it five years ago, its trailing EV-to-sales was 3.69x compared to 2.4x at the time of writing, signifying a 35% decrease while its share price declined by 20.5% in the same period. Now, its valuation multiple declining much more than the share price can be partly explained by the increase in sales but, still, given the price of gold appreciated by over 77% in the meantime as shown below, the market seems to have been rather conservative when pricing Centamin.

This could be due to investors having doubts about its future growth prospects or concerns about profitability because of country-level risks. To this end, since I last covered it, Centamin has acquired exploratory interests in the Ivory Coast (Doropo Gold Project) and Burkina Faso, but operating the Sukari mine in Egypt remains its core activity.

Looking at the industry, many gold mines are located in countries with economic uncertainty, political instability, or even civil unrest with mining owners constantly fearing that changes in government, can result in higher taxation impacting profitability or drastic changes in mining rules even disrupting operations and putting revenues under pressure. In a worst-case scenario, nationalization policies may result in the expropriation of assets.

As for Egypt, it is no stranger to civil unrest as exemplified by the Arab Spring of 2011 and this may be the reason why despite boasting higher revenue growth and profitability than AngloGold, Centamin has a lower EV to sales as shown below. Thus, country-related concentration risks may be another reason for my bullish thesis not materializing.

Pursuing further, investing in gold mining companies comes with other risks, which can impact the value of an investment. These can also be market-induced with one of them being volatility in asset prices, driven by various factors such as inflation, interest rates, and central bank policies concerning the accumulation of gold bars, without forgetting the impact of a strong U.S. dollar. Hence, as mentioned earlier, interest rates rising in 2022 made gold less attractive for investors, reducing its value and, in turn, impacting sales. This imparted volatility to Centamin's stock.

Prospects Now Exceed Risks

However, some of the above concerns are no longer justified as it will now operate within AngloGold, the world's fourth-largest miner (May 2023 figures) which has a much larger mining footprint spanning Africa, Australia, and the Americas.

Thus, with higher extraction volumes, any disruption in mining activities in one location is likely to be cushioned, at least partially, by the production capacity available elsewhere. This suggests that while sales will decline, gross margins will be less impacted given a much larger base of operations to spread fixed costs. Moreover, given the colossal amounts required to make a mine profitable, having the financial firepower of AngloGold makes it relatively easier to continue exploration activities in the Ivory Coast (Doropo Gold Project) and Burkina Faso (picture below) until they reach production phase.

Therefore, for a Centamin shareholder, the merger with AngloGold also represents diversification in the sense that it reduces concentration risks associated with producing gold in one geographical location.

Moreover, as for AngloGold, it has a much larger scale with revenue five times Centamin's, confers to it better economies of scale. This is the case both for extracting the precious metal and for exploratory (for searching for gold) infrastructure, as it can now negotiate better terms with equipment suppliers. This explains why people have been investing in AngloGold's shares as shown in the chart below, which is contrary to normal trends where the stock of the acquirer drops after an acquisition.

Note worthily, the acquisition improves AngloGold's production capabilities and market positioning, especially with the addition of Centamin's Sukari gold mine. To this end, AngloGold's gold production will be boosted by 450K ounces annually, improving its ranking in the global market.

Centamin Could Be 10% Underpriced As Gold Prices Rise

Moreover, as part of the deal, Centamin shareholders will receive $0.125 in cash and 0.06983 shares of AngloGold for each share they hold. Ultimately, they would hold 16.4% of the share capital of the merged entity.

Now, some may prefer to buy AngloGold's shares directly, but Centamin remains undervalued by around 10% ((2.65-2.4)/2.4) relative to the bigger miner as shown in the comparison table above, which is the reason why the price could appreciate further. Thus, depending on how the price of gold performs during the next few weeks until the deal is closed, the stock could potentially climb to $2.34 (2.13 x 1.1) based on the current share price of $2.13.

Moreover, Centamin held $110 million in cash versus $4 million of debt in its balance sheet at the end of the second quarter of 2024, and, looking at the regulatory process, the acquisition has received approval from Egyptian authorities and is expected to be finalized in the fourth quarter of 2024.

In conclusion, this merger provides investors with exposure to a larger, country-level diversified miner which will now boast additional production capabilities in Egypt, a country where the mining sector is still underdeveloped compared to other jurisdictions in Africa. This also comes at a time when the Egyptian government is encouraging foreign investment by putting in place favorable fiscal policy and mining laws. In this connection, the authorities aim to increase mineral mining from 1% of Egypt's GDP in 2023 to 5% by 2030 by attracting new investments to the sector, which means new dynamism and significant growth potential for miners with local presence.

At the same time, the Federal Reserve is on a path to monetary easing, the European Central Bank has cut rates by 25 basis points and eyeing more to revive the economic performance of its member states. At the same time, the Chinese Central Bank trying to accelerate economic growth through stimulus measures which include lowering rates and injecting liquidity into the system. These are three drivers that can move gold to new heights, without forgetting its value as a safe haven during periods of escalating geopolitical tensions. These can in turn drive up the stocks of miners producing the precious metal.

On a cautionary note, one should be aware that after rising by around 50% (above chart) since the beginning of this year, there are chances for gold prices to suffer from volatility, in case the inflation problem persists and central banks in the U.S. and Europe have to rethink whether to continue the process of monetary policy easing. If this happens, both Centamin's and AngloGold's share prices are likely to suffer. Finally, some more risk-averse investors may prefer to wait for a corporate announcement about the cost and revenue synergies before putting money to work.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Betting On Corporate America To 'Muddle Through' Whatever Lies Ahead
data.description:
Income Investing: Boring Can Be Beautiful

Most of my readers know by now that my approach to investing is pretty boring.

It hasn't always seemed boring because when I introduced my Income Factory strategy about a decade ago, I got a lot of push-back and criticism from readers who had been trained over many years to believe that growing their wealth over the long-term required them to invest in "growth stocks" whose total return depended primarily on achieving capital gains.

I was considered a heretic for pointing out a simple mathematical truth. If the "total return" of an asset (be it stock, bond, real estate, etc.) consists of both (1) the cash it produces during a period of time (month, year, etc.), plus (2) the capital gain or loss incurred in holding it during that time period (i.e. how much its market price goes up or down), then it makes no difference which of those two sources contributes to the total return, or by how much.

In other words, "math is math" and an asset that earns a cash yield of 10% and has a capital gain or loss of 0%, generates a 10% total return; just as much as an asset that generates a 0% cash yield, and has a capital gain of 10%. Similarly, an asset that generates 5% cash yield and increases in value by 5%; it also generates a 10% total return........and so on.

Once investors realize that, then they also realize that they have more choices for how to earn their target investment returns than they may have been told originally by their investment mentors or advisors.

Obviously, the traditional strategy of investing in growth stocks is still a good way to grow one's wealth over time. The long-term rate of return on equities, over the past century or so, has been about 9-10%. Anyone who invested in the S&P 500 index (SPY) and held it patiently since its inception 30 years ago, would have had an average annual total return of just over 10%.

To put that in perspective, a 10% total return, reinvested and compounded year after year, would double and redouble itself about every 7.2 years. So after 28 years it would have reached about 16 times its starting value, and by 35 years, it would be about 30 times its starting value.

Most of that would have been provided by capital gains, not by cash dividends. The current cash dividend yield of the S&P 500 is 1.2%. That means anyone who invests in it expecting to earn its long-term average rate of return (i.e., 10%) must be planning to achieve average annual capital gains of over 8%, in order for their total return to be between 9-10% when the two components (1.2% cash yield and 8+% capital gain) are added together.

The key word in that paragraph is "average." It is easy to pursue patiently a buy-and-hold index strategy in years when the market is cooperating, and you actually earn your average 8% capital gain. But when the market is flat or down, it can be challenging to hold tight while you are only collecting 1.2% in cash and the market value of your portfolio is going nowhere or falling. It is at that point that investors often bail out or take other "defensive" action that ends up hurting them in the long run; like going to cash or exchanging equity for more "stable" assets that end up earning a lot less.

Another Investment Theme: Sleeping At Night

It is because I am the first to admit I don't have the patience to sit quietly and collect 1.2% per annum in cash while watching my principal go nowhere or even drop in price, that I evolved to what ultimately because my Income Factory strategy. First I began buying higher yielding equities, especially utilities, infrastructure and other higher-yielding but slower-growing equities. When I did notice that my cash yields were higher than they'd been previously, but I also noticed my stocks weren't growing as much or as fast during rallies as the "growth stocks" I previously owned.

Then I began to seek out even higher yielding assets, especially credit assets. This was a natural development for me, since I'd spent much of my career in banking and credit, and knew that major institutions (banks, pension funds, endowments, etc.) were often able to earn equity or close-to-equity returns from various types of credit assets. I found many of these asset classes were available to retail investors if we looked around for them, and that they paid higher rates if we bought them in less liquid vehicles, like closed-end funds, where we had the advantage of having more assets working for us than we sometimes actually paid for (because of "discounts"), and because the fund could sometimes leverage our investment at cheap institutional interest rates and terms unavailable to the typical retail investor (more details here).

Examples of the sort of investments I'm talking about are Corporate Loan, High-Yield Bond, and other corporate credit funds like:

  • Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC) - paying a distribution yield of 9.26%
  • Invesco Senior Income Trust (VVR) - paying a distribution yield of 13%
  • XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT) - paying a distribution yield of 14.9%
  • Carlyle Credit Income Fund (CCIF) - paying a distribution yield of 15%
  • KKR Income Opportunities Fund (KIO) - paying a distribution yield of 10.2%

The result of all this was my income strategy based on the idea of achieving long-term "equity returns" of 9-10% or more by "creating our own growth" through reinvesting and compounding high cash distribution streams, and no longer being dependent on market price appreciation as our engine of growth.

I am very grateful that so many readers have accepted and embraced our strategy, not because it is any better than a traditional equity growth approach, but merely as a legitimate alternative for investors who do not want (or do not have a strong-enough stomach for) a strategy that has so much volatility from one period to the next because it depends largely on capital gains to generate its total return.

A Strategy for Troubled Times

Besides being a good alternative strategy for normal times, I think unusually uncertain times, like we are currently experiencing with our political, geopolitical and economic challenges, make a cash-dependent rather than growth-dependent strategy even more appealing. To put it more plainly: The more uncertain the overall investing environment becomes, the more I prefer credit to equity.

As described in detail elsewhere, I think of credit investments (loans, bonds, debt in general, etc.) as taking an existential risk. Will the issuer survive and pay its debts? I think of equity investing as taking an entrepreneurial risk. Will the issuer grow its earnings and dividends, and become a more valuable entity, so its stock will be worth more in the future than it was when you bought it.

What is interesting is that equity investing also includes the existential risk along with the entrepreneurial risk. An issuer can't thrive and grow its earnings and dividends if it doesn't first survive and pay its debts. So when you invest in the equity of a company, you are also taking all the risk of an investor who only buys the debt. If that company doesn't pay its loans and bonds, then its equity will be worthless.

This issue sometimes comes up when I discuss how I like to buy funds that invest in corporate loans and high-yield bonds, which often pay distribution yields in the 9-10% and higher range, and investors will say "High-yield bonds? That's JUNK. I'd never buy that, it's too risky." Then I point out how so-called "junk bonds or loans" merely means debt issued by "non-investment grade" companies, which are companies rated below BBB-. In fact, the majority of all companies are rated non-investment grade. That includes almost all mid-cap and small-cap companies. So the next time an investor tells you they would never buy high-yield bonds or loans because they are "so risky," ask them if they own any small-cap or mid-cap equity funds. In most cases, the answer is "yes, we do," which means they own the riskier equity of companies whose debt they say they'd never own.

"Skin in the Game" for CEOs = Money in the Bank for Credit Investors

Besides writing about income investing, I also wrote a book called Too Greedy for Adam Smith, which is about how excessive CEO pay has become an outrageous perversion of capitalism within the US economy. CEOs sitting on each other's boards of directors and setting each other's pay is the sort of conflict of interest that our laws and corporate governance policies, in theory, are supposed to prevent. But this huge loophole, which has allowed corporate CEO pay to skyrocket to heights that no arms-length system of compensation would ever allow, has led to our corporate leadership having far more "skin in the game" in terms of salaries, bonuses, stock options and ownership, than they ever would have in a system with real checks and balances.

Having written about this and agitated against it for years, it is clear that I do not approve of it. However, as an investor I can take advantage of it, to the extent that I rely upon it to make corporate credit investing an even safer game than it otherwise would be if CEOs only had "normal" executive salaries and pay packages.

In other words, with their huge compensation and incentive packages, I think corporate CEOs and senior management teams (who are also well paid and incentivized), will do everything they possibly can, pull every rabbit out of the hat, be as creative as they possibly can be, to keep their companies alive and well through whatever economic or political disasters they (and we) may face in the future. Regardless of what the stock market does, or whatever happens to their own company's performance and its stock (even if it has to cut its dividend, lay off employees, etc.) these highly paid and incentivized managements will find a way to "muddle through" and thus protect their own livelihoods and future prosperity.

That to me is the ultimate rationale for regarding credit as the "go to" investment for uncertain times. The math also supports this. In an "ordinary" recession, annual corporate defaults that are normally in the 1-2% range, will likely go to perhaps 5%. In a really serious recession, like the "Great Recession" of 15 years ago, defaults might go to 11-12%. With secured loans or even unsecured bonds, your likely principal recovery is likely to be about 60-65% for loans and 40-45% for bonds. So in a recession if 5% of your loans and bonds default, and you collect on average 50% of the principal, your portfolio loss is likely to be 50% times 5%, or 2.5%. In a really bad recession, maybe it will be 12% defaults with losses of 50%, or a portfolio loss of 6%.

I suspect that the impact on an equity portfolio would be far greater than that, in the same environment.

I look forward to your comments and questions.
